storm.summary_layperson: Thunderstorms with some severe potential are moving slowly eastward from New Jersey, but the cool Long Island Sound is likely to weaken them before they reach the cove — expect increased winds tonight and possible distant rumbles, while towns a few miles inland take the brunt.
storm.local_effect_blurb: Cool Sound water (70°F vs. 97°F air — a 26°F gap) creates a strong stable marine layer that typically chokes incoming convection before it crosses the shoreline.

arrival.blurb: Radar-tracked cells ~40 mi due west closing at 3.6 kt; the marine layer is likely to push arrival late and weaken any storms before they cross the Sound.

storm.detail_html:
Scattered thunderstorms with some severe potential are organized across NJ and the NY metro area, 40–47 miles WSW of the cove. KOKX radar shows a cluster of cells with peak reflectivity of 56 dBZ, moving east-southeast at roughly 5 kt — an unusually slow pace. The KOKX Area Forecast Discussion notes a severe thunderstorm watch for western portions of the area, and SPC has placed the region in a Slight Risk for severe weather today (eastern CT/Long Island at Marginal Risk). HRRR CAPE is 1,710 J/kg (RAP at 2,480 J/kg) with 0–6 km shear of ~20 kt — enough for vigorous storms, though not yet in the strongly organized range.

The dominant local modifier is a strong marine stable layer over Long Island Sound. At 70°F, the Sound water is 27°F cooler than the 97°F inland air — one of the largest air-water differentials of the season. Cells approaching from the WSW must cross the Sound nearly perpendicularly, maximizing exposure to this stabilizing layer. CAPE at 1,710 J/kg is well below the 3,000 J/kg punch-through threshold, and shear at 20 kt is well below the 35 kt amplifying gate — both parameters favor storm suppression rather than intensification at the coast.

Most likely outcome for the cove: winds pick up this evening as the system approaches (expect W/WNW gusts), but cells are likely to weaken or drop their rain as they cross the Sound. The cove may see a brief gust increase and possible distant thunder overnight, while areas 2+ miles inland take a heavier hit. This is the classic cove-spared, inland-soaked pattern when the marine layer is this strong.

What would change the call: if CAPE climbs toward the 2,480 J/kg RAP estimate or higher, the marine-layer protection erodes. Watch for cells consolidating west of Newark into a compact MCS — linear squall-line dynamics can override local suppression. A shift in storm motion to due east (vs. ESE) would also reduce the effective Sound-crossing distance and deflection time.

Cells are closing very slowly at 3–4 kt. Radar-tracked ETA to the cove is around 2:55 AM EDT, with a wide arrival window of 12:31 AM–7:13 AM — the broad late edge reflects both slow-motion uncertainty and the marine layer's tendency to push arrival late or prevent it entirely. Any overnight arrival is most likely a wind event; significant rain or lightning at the cove is the lower-probability outcome given the strong Sound effect.

hourly.shift_note: steady W — cove running ~4 kt above model (RYC 12 kt now)
backing to WSW near sunset (~8:23 PM) — thermal relaxing

wind.tactics_blurb: W/WNW winds 8–9 kt (cove running ~4 kt stronger per RYC) oscillating ±20° through the evening; expect a backing shift to WSW near sunset (~8:23 PM), then a persistent NW rotation after dark as the thermal collapses — start center and be ready for the pre-sunset left shift.
wind.tactics_detail_html:
Today's wind regime is B-moderate — synoptic westerly with thermal amplification. The inland heating is extraordinary (97°F air vs. 70°F Sound water, a 26°F delta), creating a strong thermal pressure gradient, but the flow has organized along the synoptic W/WNW axis rather than the classic SSW sea-breeze direction. Only 32% of coastal stations are blowing southerly, confirming no classic sea breeze is established. Mean authoritative speed across airport anemometers is 8.8 kt; RYC is reading 11.9 kt WNW (294°) with gusts to 19.1 kt — the cove is channeling and funneling W/WNW flow through its southern opening.

HRRR/obs divergence is notable: the model shows W 270° at 7.9 kt for 5 PM, while RYC reads WNW 294° at 11.9 kt — 4 kt and 24° apart. Trust RYC for the current hour. The rightward bias relative to HRRR is consistent with cove channeling of WNW flow and thermally-driven surface convergence adding speed. For forecast hours, use HRRR trends as directional guidance while expecting actual cove speeds to run 3–5 kt above model values. No direction correction is applied (B regime, not A/A+); raw HRRR directions are used for the forecast hours.

Direction character through the evening: W (270°) veering to WNW (282–289°) through 7 PM, then a notable backing to WSW (252°) around sunset (~8:23 PM) as the land-heating gradient relaxes, followed by a swing to NW (307°) by 10 PM as the nocturnal boundary layer stabilizes. Expect roughly 60° of oscillation across the window, with the WSW dip being the most tactically significant shift.

Cove geography in W/WNW flow: the wind channels in from the NYC direction — steady direction but holes near the CT (eastern/northern) shoreline, with the best pressure mid-cove and toward the cove mouth. In oscillating W/WNW conditions the course geometry is fairly symmetric — no strong persistent pressure advantage to either side.

Racing tactics: favor center starts to preserve options in both oscillating directions. On the first beat a slight right-side lean takes you toward the dominant WNW source. Watch carefully for the pre-sunset backing to WSW around 8:15–8:30 PM — that is a port-tack lift (starboard header) that briefly rewards the left side. If racing past 8:30 PM, treat the NW shift as a persistent rightward rotation signaling thermal collapse, not an oscillation to play back. Speeds are powered up (10–12 kt range at the cove) — solid planing conditions for skiffs. B-moderate gradient wind holds through sunset and into the evening; no full wind collapse expected.

data_caveats: Buoy 44040 wind observation 73 min old (last updated 8:15 PM UTC) — use with caution
EW9204 (Valhalla APRS) stale 416 min — excluded from ensemble
KISP (Islip) and KFRG (Farmingdale) anemometers down — LI south shore wind obs unavailable
KPVD (Providence) anemometer data null — backdoor sentinel degraded to 2 of 3 stations
EW9544 (Old Greenwich) reporting 0 kt amid active W/WNW winds — possible anemometer issue, speed excluded from ensemble
AFD notes severe thunderstorm watch for western portions of the area; not reflected in local NWS alerts feed for this lat/lon — watch likely applies to areas west of Greenwich
